result:
The second hand is having an affair with the minute hand; I can tell by the way they stutter across the kitchen clock, pausing just long enough to let guilt settle into the gears. My coffee has gone cold, but I keep sipping it because the mug was hers—one of those hand-thrown ceramics we bought in a drizzle-soaked town whose name I can’t pronounce anymore. The glaze is cracked like a spiderweb, and if I press my thumb into the fissure I can almost feel the pulse of the day we argued about whether spiders dream. She said yes, because why else would they rebuild the same web in the same corner after you sweep it away? I said no, because instinct is just a hallway with no doors. We never finished the conversation; the taxi arrived, the airport swallowed her, and now the only thing traveling is steam ghosting out of the drain.

I keep thinking the refrigerator is humming in Morse code, but when I kneel and put my ear to the vent, it’s only the ordinary heart murmur of coolant and regret. Still, I answer: “I’m still here.” The appliance does not reply, yet the light inside flickers once, like a drunk wink, and for a moment I believe that every object in the house is conspiring to keep her absence warm. The couch cushions retain the exact angle of her last curl-up, a comma suggesting the sentence will continue, though the paragraph has ended. I sit in the dent, feel the foam attempt its slow resurrection, and decide that furniture is more optimistic than people.

Outside, the city is practicing a new dialect of darkness. Streetlamps flicker in prime-number sequences—2, 3, 5, 11—then go black entirely. Somewhere a car alarm learns how to yodel. I open the window because the night air smells of burnt sugar and thunder, a combination that once preceded the first kiss she gave me during a power outage. The kiss tasted of candle smoke and the last candle itself, waxen and bright. Now the darkness is digitized; pixels of storm crowd the horizon, each one a deleted photograph neither of us thought to recover from the cloud. I consider sticking my head out and drinking the weather like a flaming shot, but the neighbors already think I’m the guy who tried to teach the stray cats solfège. (Only partly true: I merely sang to them, and they judged me silently, tails curled like skeptical eyebrows.)

I read once that astronauts lose ten percent of their blood volume in space because the heart grows lazy without gravity’s daily arguments. The same must happen to hearts left without the ballast of a beloved’s toothbrush. Mine is sloshing around like an underfilled water balloon, seeking corners that don’t exist. To compensate I walk the hallway in figure eights, the infinity symbol, hoping to centrifuge some red back into the ventricles. On every third lap I pass the mirror and notice my reflection lagging a frame behind, as though buffering. I wave; it waves back a half-second late, forever out of sync, the way conversations now happen only inside my skull where she answers immediately, correctly, affectionately—except the bandwidth is all nostalgia and no flesh.

At 3:07 a.m. the smoke detector decides its battery is existential and chirps. The sound is tiny but drills straight into the tympanic membrane like a bird asking for directions it will never follow. I drag the step-stool from under the lightbulb graveyard in the pantry and climb. When I twist the detector off the ceiling, a postcard falls out from behind it—sun-bleached, edges mottled like antique parchment. On the front: a beach whose sand is the exact color of her shoulders the summer she turned twenty-nine. On the back: her handwriting, a careless stream of ink that says, “If you find this, we’ve either moved or I’ve forgotten why I hid it. Either way, the ocean is still real.” No signature, just a tiny doodle of a compass rose with the north arrow pointing off the card entirely. I sit on the top step of the stool, a king of nothing, and hold the postcard against my chest until the detector chirps again, embarrassed by the silence.

I replace the battery with one labeled “guaranteed until 2038,” a year that sounds like science fiction and feels like a dare. While I’m up there, I notice the ceiling has a hairline crack shaped remarkably like the flight path we took from New York to Lisbon, the one where we drank tiny bottles of wine and she let me draw constellations on the inside of her wrist, claiming the plane’s cabin lights were inadequate for stargazing. The crack is growing as I watch, a slow-motion lightning bolt, and I wonder if the house is remembering for me because my own brain has begun to overwrite sectors. I speak aloud: “You can’t just archive a person.” The crack does not respond, but the insulation sighs.

Downstairs, I open the junk drawer—our Museum of Almost—and fish out the spare key she swore she’d never need because she’d never leave. It’s still warm, as if recently held. I weigh it in my palm: fourteen grams of brass, a thousand tons of possibility. The key goes to a lock that might no longer exist, or might be buried in the sediment of another city, another hallway. I close my fist around it until the teeth imprint a tiny skyline across my lifeline, a new topography to navigate.

The coffee maker chooses that instant to gurgle, though I never refilled it. A ghost brew, black as the inside of a closed piano. I pour a cup anyway, add nothing—no milk, no sugar, no prayer—because bitterness is the only honest flavor left. Steam curls in the shape of her name, then dissolves, obeying the second law of thermodynamics and the first law of grief: that order is always borrowed, never owned.

I carry the mug to the back step. The moon is a paper cutout snagged on the telephone wire, and every dog in the neighborhood is singing the same minor seventh, a chord that in some cultures means welcome, in others goodbye. I raise my cup to the sky, an informal treaty, and drink the impossible coffee. It tastes of burnt wires and the last time we laughed so hard we dropped the watermelon on the kitchen tiles, pink juice racing into the grout, making stains that resembled maps of imaginary archipelagos. We tried to name them but could only come up with puns that worked in the past tense: “Coulda-Baja,” “Shoulda-Loa,” “Didn’t-Diego.” Now the islands are shrinking beneath my tongue, swallowed by the ocean of bitter.

When the cup is empty, I see the bottom reveals a secret message fired into the ceramic: “Rotate 180° for perspective.” I invert the mug; the drips rearrange themselves into a new pattern, and suddenly her absence is no longer a hole but a door. I have no idea if it opens inward or outward, but I hear the hinge creak—maybe the house stretching, maybe my own knees—regardless, it’s a sound like the beginning of something. I place the mug on the step, bottom up, an offering to whatever gravity keeps us circling the same drain.

Inside again, the clock’s hands have aligned, pretending nothing happened. I stand in front of it, place my palm over the glass, and feel the ticking push against my skin like a kitten wanting in. I whisper: “Keep going. I’ll catch up.” The second hand twitches, embarrassed by sentiment, yet it picks up speed, racing now, frantic to outrun the minute hand, to cheat time the way memory cheats death—by replaying so often the tape grows thin, translucent, until light shines right through it and you can see tomorrow on the other side.

I think I’ll sweep the cobweb from the corner tomorrow, but leave the spider. Everyone deserves a place to dream.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
